videos.title: Estimating Lengths | Guess and Measure for Kids
videos.description: Learn how to estimate the length of objects before measuring them. Use your hand span, arm length, and footsteps to make smart guesses!
learningObjectives: Use body parts as measurement benchmarks
keyConceptsCovered: One big step is about 1 m

videos.title: Units of Length | Centimetres, Metres, and Kilometres for Kids
videos.description: Learn about the three main units of length: centimetres (cm), metres (m), and kilometres (km). Discover when to use each unit with fun everyday examples!
learningObjectives: Choose the correct unit for estimation
Relate objects to the right unit

videos.title: What is Measurement? | Introduction to Measuring for Kids
videos.description: Learn what measurement means and why we measure things. Understand that measurement tells us how long, how heavy, or how much something is.
learningObjectives: Know when estimation is enough and when exact measurement is needed

videos.title: Length Word Problems | Solving Measurement Problems Step by Step
videos.description: Solve fun word problems about measuring length! Learn how to add, subtract, and compare lengths in real-life situations like measuring cloth and roads.
learningObjectives: Use estimation to check word problem answers
Apply estimation skills to real problems

excellent_performance.description: For students who scored >= 90% on readiness
excellent_performance.rationale: Quick estimation introduction and unit review for strong students

ready_performance.description: For students who scored 80-89% on readiness
ready_performance.rationale: Complete estimation coverage with word problem application

partial_readiness.description: For students who scored 50-79% on readiness
partial_readiness.rationale: Measurement review first, then estimation concepts

after_prerequisite_review.description: For students who scored < 50% but completed prerequisites

questions.question: About how long is a new pencil?
questions.explanation: A new pencil is about 18 cm long. That is less than your ruler! It would not be 2 cm (too small), 1 m (too big), or 5 km (way too big).

usageNotes.teacherGuidance: Make estimation a fun game! Show objects and ask students to guess the length before measuring. Keep a class chart of estimates vs actual measurements.
usageNotes.parentGuidance: Play the estimation game at home: guess how long something is, then measure together. This builds number sense and measurement intuition.
usageNotes.studentGuidance: Estimation is like being a measurement detective! Use clues like your hand span (about 15 cm) and your arm span (about 1 m) to make smart guesses.
